coredata.dc:title: Dual-mode ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric sensor for rapid visual detection of Hg2+ using poly(T)-tailed ssDNA-silver nanoclusters
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Yu
Zhang, Ying
Wang, Hui Zhu
Jiang, Meng Wei
Gui, Guo Feng
Fu, Dong
Ren, Wang
coredata.dc:description: Rapid, sensitive, and accurate detection of heavy metal ions is significant for human health and ecological security. Herein, a novel single-stranded DNA with poly(thymidine) tail is tactfully designed as template to synthesize dual-emission silver nanoclusters (ssDNA-AgNCs). The obtained AgNCs simultaneously emit red and green fluorescence, and the red emission can be selectively quenched by Hg2+, meanwhile the green emission of AgNCs increases synchronously. Thus ssDNA-AgNCs as a single probe shows excellent ratiometric fluorescence sensing for Hg2+ with a detection limit of 0.2 nM, and Hg2+ as low as 10.0 nM can be fluorescently identified by naked eye within 5 min. Moreover, the proposed nanoprobe also exhibits a good ratiometric colorimetric sensing for Hg2+, and the obvious color change of nanoprobe also enables a rapid and visual monitoring of Hg2+ under visible light. The dual mode ratiometric response of Hg2+ can be ascribed to the rapid redox reaction between Hg2+ and Ag0 on the surface of AgNCs and the subsequent formation of silver amalgam. The resultant dual-mode ratiometric sensor has been successfully applied to the determination of Hg2+ in environmental water samples. This study provides a new strategy to synthesize dual-emission AgNCs by scientifically designing terminus sequence of ssDNA template, and develops a facile and efficient single-probe and dual-mode ratiometric sensor for visual monitoring of Hg2+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-emission AgNCs
Ratiometric sensor
Dual-mode visual detection
Hg2+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Introduction
Material and methods
Reagents
Apparatus
Synthesis and characterization of ssDNA-templated AgNCs
Fluorimetric and colorimetric detection of Hg2+
Analysis of Hg2+ in environmental water samples
Results and discussion
Effect of ssDNA terminal base type and length on the fluorescence spectra of AgNCs
The role of T10 tail in regulating spectra properties of ssDNA-templated AgNCs
Feasibility of T10-SP-AgNCs as nanoprobe for dual-mode sensing of Hg2+ and its possible response mechanism
Optimal conditions for the preparation of nanoprobe and the fluorescence sensing of Hg2+
Sensitivity of the dual-mode ratiometric and visual sensor for Hg2+
Selectivity, repeatability and stability of dual-mode ratiometric sensor
Practicability of the proposed sensor for Hg2+ in environmental water samples
Conclusion
CRediT authorship contribution statement
Acknowledgments
Supplementary data
References

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work received support from the Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province (No. 2022NSFSC0103), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22064005), the Key Laboratory of Low-cost Rural Environmental Treatment Technology at Sichuan University of Arts and Science Education Department of Sichuan Province (XCH2022ZB-04), the Undergraduate Innovation Foundation of Sichuan Province (S20221062209), and the Innovation Fund of Postgraduate, Sichuan University of Science & Engineering (Y2023058, Y2024080).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Base sequence of different ssDNA templates (The black bold C-rich sequences are used to chelate Ag+; the red letters represent the terminal sequence of ssDNA template for regulating the fluorescence emission properties of AgNCs); (B) Two-dimensional fluorescence contour maps of AgNCs synthesized by nine different ssDNA sequence templates.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra and (B) visible absorption spectra of AgNCs synthesized with different DNA template (a: T10-SP-AgNCs; b: SP-AgNCs; c: T10 + SP-AgNCs); (C) TEM image and (D) particle size distribution of T10-SP-AgNCs; (E) TEM image and (F) particle size distribution of SP-AgNCs. The insets of C and E are the high-magnified TEM image of single AgNC.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of T10-SP-AgNCs (a) in the absence and (b and c) presence of Hg2+ with different concentration, and inset is the corresponding fluorescence photograph under 365 nm UV lamp; (B) Visible absorption spectra of T10-SP-AgNCs (a) before and (b) after the addition of 200.0 nM Hg2+, and inset is the corresponding colorimetric photograph under visible light; (C) XPS C 1 s, N 1 s, B 1 s, P 2p, Ag 3d and Hg 4f spectra of T10-SP-AgNCs in the absence and presence of 200.0 nM Hg2+, and inset is high-resolution XPS Hg(0) 4f spectra; (D) High-resolution XPS Ag0 and Ag+ 3d spectra of T10-SP-AgNCs in the absence (a) and in the presence (b) of 200.0 nM Hg2+, respectively; (E) CD spectra of T10-SP-AgNCs before and after the addition of 200.0 nM Hg2+ at pH 6.6, respectively; (F) HRTEM image of T10-SP-AgNCs in the presence of 200.0 nM Hg2+, and inset is the particle size distribution of T10-SP-AgNCs; (G) Fluorescence spectra of T10-SP-AgNCs whether to add 2.0 mM EDTA after addition of 200.0 nM Hg2+, respectively; (H) Fluorescence spectra of T10-SP-AgNCs whether to add 2.0 mM EDTA before addition of 200.0 nM Hg2+, respectively.

(A) Dual-emission fluorescence spectra of AgNCs nanoprobe containing different C Hg 2+; Insets are the corresponding fluorescence photographs under 365 nm UV lamp; (B) The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (F 626/F 535) and C Hg 2+; Insets are the relevant equation of linear regression and coefficient correlation; (C) Absorption spectra of AgNCs nanoprobe containing different C Hg 2+; Insets are the corresponding colorimetric photographs under visible light; (D) The linear relationship between absorbance ratio (A 550/A 430) and C Hg 2+; Insets are the relevant equation of linear regression and coefficient correlation.

(A) Selective experiments of fluorimetric ratiometric sensor for Hg2+; Insets are the corresponding fluorescence photographs under 365 nm UV lamp; (B) Selective experiments of colorimetric ratiometric sensor for Hg2+; Insets are the corresponding colorimetric photographs under visible light; F 0 and A 0 are the signal ratio of F 626/F 535 and A550/A430 of blank solution, respectively. F 626/F 535 and A 550/A 430 are the signal ratio of sample solution, respectively. C Hg 2+ is 125.0 nM, and C Fe 3+ is 2.5 μM, and C Ag + is 1.25 μM, and C Cu 2+ is 625.0 nM; The concentration of Cr3+, Ni2+, Bi3+, Cd2+ and Zn2+ is 6.0 μM, and the concentration of Eu3+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Pb2+, and Ba2+ is 12.0 μM, respectively.

(A) Schematic representation of synthesizing poly(T)-terminal ssDNA-AgNCs and its application for sensing Hg2+. (B) Schematic illustration of dual-mode ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric sensor for rapid visual sensing of Hg2+ using ssDNA-AgNCs as single-probe.

Schematic illustration of the reaction process and possible fluorescence response mechanism of T10-SP-AgNCs nanoprobe (A) when addition of Hg2+ and subsequent EDTA and (B) when addition of EDTA and subsequent Hg2+; (C) Schematic diagram of the possible physicochemical reaction between trace Hg2+ and Ag0 on the surface of T10-SP-AgNCs.

head.ce:title: Dual-mode ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric sensor for rapid visual detection of Hg2+ using poly(T)-tailed ssDNA-silver nanoclusters

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yu Zou and Ying Zhang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid, sensitive, and accurate detection of heavy metal ions is significant for human health and ecological security. Herein, a novel single-stranded DNA with poly(thymidine) tail is tactfully designed as template to synthesize dual-emission silver nanoclusters (ssDNA-AgNCs). The obtained AgNCs simultaneously emit red and green fluorescence, and the red emission can be selectively quenched by Hg2+, meanwhile the green emission of AgNCs increases synchronously. Thus ssDNA-AgNCs as a single probe shows excellent ratiometric fluorescence sensing for Hg2+ with a detection limit of 0.2 nM, and Hg2+ as low as 10.0 nM can be fluorescently identified by naked eye within 5 min. Moreover, the proposed nanoprobe also exhibits a good ratiometric colorimetric sensing for Hg2+, and the obvious color change of nanoprobe also enables a rapid and visual monitoring of Hg2+ under visible light. The dual mode ratiometric response of Hg2+ can be ascribed to the rapid redox reaction between Hg2+ and Ag0 on the surface of AgNCs and the subsequent formation of silver amalgam. The resultant dual-mode ratiometric sensor has been successfully applied to the determination of Hg2+ in environmental water samples. This study provides a new strategy to synthesize dual-emission AgNCs by scientifically designing terminus sequence of ssDNA template, and develops a facile and efficient single-probe and dual-mode ratiometric sensor for visual monitoring of Hg2+.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury pollutant seriously threatens the ecosystems and human health due to its bioaccumulative and non-degradable effect, and high toxicity at low concentrations [1–3]. Mercuric ion (Hg2+) is one of the main and stable forms of mercury pollution [4]. Visual detection of trace Hg2+ is particularly suitable for routine analysis and field real-time monitoring, which is significant for protecting human health and ecological security. Typical colorimetric methods [5] and fluorescence methods [6] are convenient for visual detection. In order to improve visual accuracy and reliability, many ratiometric colorimetric or fluorescent sensors have been studied based on various signal probes such as carbon dots (CDs) [7,8], organic dyes [9], MOFs materials [10,11], biomolecules-stabilized nanomaterials [12] and DNA-templated nanomaterials [13–15]. For instance, dithiothreitol and agar-stabilized Au@Ag nanoparticles were used as colorimetric probe for ratiometric detection of Hg2+ [16]. Blue-emissive CDs and yellow-emissive CdTe quantum dots were employed as fluorescent probes for ratiometric sensing of Hg2+ [17]. CDs and metalloporphyrin were used as fluorescent probes for ratiometric assay of Hg2+ [18]. DNA-functionalized and dye-loaded CDs were applied to ratiometric fluorescence detection of Hg2+ [19]. Aptamer-modified Cu@Au nanoclusters and the extra two complementary DNA sequences were combined for ratiometric fluorescence sensing of Hg2+ [20]. DNA-templated Au nanoclusters (AgNCs) and the other complementary DNA sequence have been designed as fluorescence nanoprobe for ratiometric assay of Hg2+ due to the formation of T-Hg2+-T complexs [21]. Among these signal probes DNA-templated AgNCs are attractive because the optical properties of AgNCs can be adjusted by DNA sequence types, structures and circumstances [22–25]. Recently, Dong et al. regulated fluorescence color of DNA-templated AgNCs by using supplementary DNA and nonfluorescent AgNCs, achieving the ratiometric detection of DNA targets [26]. Most of the DNA-templated AgNCs sensors usually employed at least two probes or primers to output single-mode signal, and required sophisticated and tedious probe preparation and high cost. It is well-known that multi-mode assay approach provides multiple parameters and parallel information of a single analytical system, which simplifies significantly the assay procedures and saves assay time and efficiently avoids false positive or false negative results [27]. On the other hand, some reports indicated that A and T bases around AgNC core could effect fluorescence intensity of AgNCs [26], and the fluorescence intensity or color of DNA-templated AgNCs can also be changed by the proximity of poly G, poly C, or complementary DNA sequence-templated AgNCs [21]. As far as we known, the possible multicolor fluorescence of ssDNA-templated AgNCs has not been systematically studied by regulating terminal base sequence of ssDNA. To date, very few ssDNA-templated AgNCs have been reported as a single probe to fabricate multi-mode ratiometric sensor. Thus it is attractive and challenging to develop multi-emission AgNCs by modulating terminal base sequence of ssDNA, and it is significant to construct a single-probe and multi-mode ratiometric sensor based on ssDNA-templated AgNCs.

In this study, we investigated the effects of ssDNA sequences and terminal base types/length on optical properties of AgNCs in detail, and then selected a superior poly(T)-tailed C-rich ssDNA as template to synthesize dual-emission fluorescent AgNCs (ssDNA-AgNCs) via one-pot and one-step method (Scheme 1 A). Inspired by the redox reaction between Ag0 and Hg2+ adsorption onto Ag nanoparticles [28], the optimal ssDNA-AgNCs have been explored as probe for trace Hg2+ assay. As shown in Scheme 1B, the red fluorescence of ssDNA-AgNCs can be selectively quenched by Hg2+ within 5 min, and its green fluorescence was synchronously promoted. Meanwhile, Hg2+ can also trigger the ratiometric colorimetric response of AgNCs due to the redox reaction between Ag0 and Hg2+ and the subsequent formation of Ag amalgam nanoparticles. Herein, the ssDNA-AgNCs as a single probe achieved dual-mode ratiometric sensing and rapid visual identification of trace Hg2+ (Scheme 1B). The possible optical response mechanism was further explored and clarified by spectroscopy and transmission electron microscopy characteristics.

All of ssDNA templates were synthesized by Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and purified by HPLC. DNA sequence information in this work was listed in Fig. 1 A. AgNO3, Hg(NO3)2, Cr(NO3)3, Ca(NO3)2, CdSO4, Eu(NO3)3, Fe(NO3)3, Zn(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, Bi(NO3)3 and Tris-(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane (Tris) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Ba(NO3)2, Mg(NO3)2, Cu(NO3)2, Co(NO3)3, Ni(NO3)2 and acetic acid (HAc) were from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). NaBH4 was purchased from Chengdu Jinshan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). All the ssDNA templates was dissolved in Tris-HAc buffer (20 mM, pH 6.6) and stored at 4 °C for further use. All chemical reagents used in this study were of analytical grade. Double distilled water (ddH2O) as experimental water was used in this study.

RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was used to test fluorescence spectra. Ultraviolet–visible spectra (UV–vis) were obtained using UV-2600i spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). FEI Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI, USA) high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) was used to characterize the particle size and morphology of AgNCs at 200 kV. Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (Thermo, USA) X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) was used to record XPS spectra. The circular dichroism (CD) spectra were obtained by JASCO J-1499 circular dichroism spectropolarimeter (Jasco, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of ssDNA-templated AgNCs
ce:section.ce:para: DNA-templated AgNCs were synthesized according to a previous reports with some modifications [29,30]. In brief, 20.0 μL ssDNA aqueous solution (100.0 μM), and 48.0 μL AgNO3 solution (1.0 mM) were added into 980.0 μL Tris-HAc buffer solution (20.0 mM, pH 6.6). The coordination reaction of mixed solution was proceeded at room temperature (25 ± 1 °C) in the dark for 1 h. Then fresh 24.0 μL NaBH4 solution (1.0 mM) was added into the mixture, which was incubated in the dark at 25 °C for 6 h. The obtained ssDNA-templated AgNCs (ssDNA-AgNCs) was stored at 4 °C for further use. The final molar ratio of DNA, AgNO3 and NaBH4 was 1: 24: 12.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorimetric and colorimetric detection of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: For fluorimetric and colorimetric measurements, ssDNA-AgNCs solution (80.0 μL) was mixed with Tris-HAc buffer (100.0 μL), and different concentration of Hg2+ was subsequently added into nanoprobe solution, and then diluted with ddH2O to 200.0 μL. The mixtures were kept at room temperature for 5 min, and then UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were recorded. The fluorescence emission spectra of final solutions were recorded with excitation wavelengths of 440 nm and 560 nm, respectively. For control solutions, sample solutions, and selectivity experiments, the analysis procedures were similar. All the spectral measurements operated at room temperature. All fluorescence photographs were recorded under a 365 nm UV lamp by Canon camera, and all colorimetric photographs were recorded under visible light by Canon camera. Calibration curves were obtained according to Hg2+ concentration (C Hg 2+) and the corresponding absorbance or fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Hg2+ in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water and Fuxi River water (Zigong) samples were collected as the environmental water samples to assess the practicality of proposed ratiometric sensor for Hg2+ assay. The collected water samples were filtrated with filtration membrane with pore size of 0.22 μm. The filtrate was treated with high-speed centrifugation at 12 000 rpm for 10 min to remove any suspended particles. Finally, the supernatant was used for the fluorescence and colorimetric analysis of Hg2+. Appropriate amount of Hg2+ standard solutions were spiked into the real water sample solutions (10.0 μL) for the recovery experiments. ssDNA-AgNCs solution (80.0 μL) was mixed with Tris-HAc buffer (100.0 μL) as nanoprobe solution. The real water sample solutions (10.0 μL) and appropriate amount of Hg2+ standard solutions were spiked into nanoprobe solution, and then diluted with ddH2O to 200.0 μL. The mixtures were kept at room temperature for 5 min, and then UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ssDNA terminal base type and length on the fluorescence spectra of AgNCs
ce:section.ce:para: Nine different template ssDNA sequences were designed based on the reported literature with major adjustment [25,26]. The ssDNA sequence information is listed in Fig. 1A, and the black bold C-rich sequence acts as the chelating area of Ag+, and the red terminal sequences are designed to regulate the fluorescence properties of AgNCs. As exhibited in Fig. 1B, when C base homopolymer (C12), C-rich ssDNA sequence (SP), and poly (C)-terminal SP sequence (C5-SP) are used as template to synthesize AgNCs, and these AgNCs just emit faint red fluorescence. Remarkably, when the other three types of 5-nt base homopolymer tail were designed in SP sequence as template (A5-SP, G5-SP and T5-SP), dual-emission AgNCs were synthesized. G5-SP-templated AgNCs emit two adjacent red fluorescence. Both T5-SP-templated AgNCs and A5-SP-templated AgNCs emit well-separated green and red fluorescence, and the former shows stronger dual-emission fluorescence. Furthermore, poly(T) tail with different base length were further designed in SP sequence as template (T10-SP, T15-SP, T20-SP) for AgNCs formation, respectively. All these poly(T)-tailed SP-templated AgNCs show green and red dual-emission fluorescence, and the two fluorescence emissions are well separated. In particular, T10-SP-templated AgNCs (T10-SP-AgNCs) emit strongest red fluorescence, and the red fluorescence intensity is approximately 4 times than that of SP-templated AgNCs. Thus, T10-SP was chosen as a superior ssDNA template to synthesize dual-emission AgNCs (T10-SP-AgNCs) for further application.

Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra of T10-SP-AgNCs revealed a maximum excitation wavelength of 440 nm and a maximum emission wavelength of 535 nm for green fluorescence (Fig. S1A). Meanwhile, the maximum excitation and emission wavelength for red fluorescence was 560 nm and 626 nm (Fig. S1B), respectively. Moreover, a single excitation wavelength of 440 nm can indeed generate dual emissions at 535 nm and 626 nm (Fig. S1C). However, the intensity of the red emission at 626 nm was too weak (solid curve in Fig. S1C) to detect trace Hg2+ (dash-dot curve in Fig. S1C). In order to accomplish the sensitive detection of trace Hg2+ in this study, two maximum excitation wavelengths (440 nm and 560 nm) were employed to obtain red and green emission, respectively.

On the basis of the above experimental results and the previous reports [31–34], we infer that the moderate length and flexible poly(T) tails absorb onto the nanocluster core and prevent partly reduction of Ag+, resulting in a local high proportion of Ag+ within the AgNCs, which is responsible for green emission. The red fluorescence of AgNCs originates from the fluorophores containing a higher proportion of Ag0. That is to say, the red emission of AgNCs is attributed to reduced silver clusters, and the green emission is ascribed to oxidized nanoclusters [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of T10 tail in regulating spectra properties of ssDNA-templated AgNCs
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, T10-tailed SP sequence, SP sequence, and the mixture of free T10 and SP sequence were used as template to synthesize AgNCs at the same condition, and the prepared AgNCs were denoted as T10-SP-AgNCs, SP-AgNCs and T10 + SP-AgNCs, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2 , only T10-SP-AgNCs (curve a) emit strongest dual-emissions fluorescence (red line, Fig. 2A) and three remarkable absorption peaks (Fig. 2B). Both SP-AgNCs (curve b, blue dotted line) and T10 + SP-AgNCs (curve c, green dotted line) show very similar and inferior single red fluorescence (Fig. 2A) and two absorption peaks (Fig. 2B). By comparison with SP-AgNCs, T10 + SP-AgNCs appear weaker fluorescence emission and absorbance, indicating free T10 sequence was useless and even hindered the optical properties of AgNCs. Among the three AgNCs, T10-SP-AgNCs exhibit a new absorption peak at around 500 nm (circled in black, curve a in Fig. 2B) and a strongest absorbance at 430 nm and 550 nm (Fig. 1B). According to the principle of complementary color light, the absorption peak at 430 nm of AgNCs is the characteristic absorption of yellow solution, and the absorption peaks at 500 nm and 550 nm belong to the characteristic absorption of red solution. Moreover, the absorption peak at 430 nm of AgNCs is consistent with the maximum excitation wavelength of its green fluorescence (Fig. S1A), and the absorption peak at 550 nm is consistent with the maximum excitation wavelength of red fluorescence (Fig. S1B). Therefore, the absorption peak at 430 nm of AgNCs can be assigned to the oxidized nanoclusters and the surface plasmon resonance peak of silver nanoparticles [26,35]. Meanwhile, the absorption peak at 500 nm and 550 nm of AgNCs can be assigned to the reduced nanoclusters. The above spectra results indicated that T10 tail in SP sequence played significant role in regulating dual-emission fluorescence and visible light absorption of AgNCs.

Furthermore, the particle size and morphology of SP-AgNCs and T10-SP-AgNCs were characterized by HRTEM. T10-SP-AgNCs exhibit spherical and uniform distribution (Fig. 2C), and the average diameter is 2.63 ± 0.46 nm (Fig. 2D), but the average diameter of SP-templated AgNCs is 4.81 ± 0.76 nm (Fig. 2E and F). Moreover, T10-SP-AgNCs (inset of Fig. 2C) and SP-AgNCs (inset of Fig. 2E) showed respective lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 and 0.23 nm, which can be ascribed to the (111) lattice plane of Ag crystal [36]. The above TEM characteristics indicated T10-SP-AgNCs showed better dispersion and smaller particle size than SP-AgNCs.

In brief, T10-SP-AgNCs showed excellent fluorescence dual-emission, characteristic visible absorption peaks, well dispersion, and small particle size. It may be ascribed to the controllable proximity effect of terminal T10 sequence in ssDNA template. Flexible T10 tail of template ssDNA also can coordinated with Ag+ [37] and provide external assistance for adjacent AgNCs, which facilitated the stability of AgNCs and greatly improved the morphology of AgNCs and optical properties, especially red-emission fluorophore.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of T10-SP-AgNCs as nanoprobe for dual-mode sensing of Hg2+ and its possible response mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the possible reducibility of AgNCs [38,39], Hg2+ was chose as an oxidant model to investigate the fluorescence and colorimetric responses of T10-SP-AgNCs. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence intensity (FI) of T10-SP-AgNCs (curve a) at 626 nm sharply decreases and that at 535 nm increases in the presence of Hg2+ with different concentrations (curves b and c). The fluorescence color of nanoprobe correspondingly changed from red to yellow and green with increasing concentration of Hg2+ under 365 nm UV light (Inset of Fig. 3A). Meanwhile, Hg2+ resulted in a sharply decreased absorbance of nanoprobe at 550 nm and a simultaneous increased absorbance at 430 nm (Fig. 3B). Consequently, the pink color of nanoprobe solution (photograph a, inset of Fig. 3B) becomes pale yellow in the presence of Hg2+ (photograph b, inset of Fig. 3B). The above experimental results showed that Hg2+ can effectively trigger both fluorescence and colorimetric response of nanoprobe. Therefore, it is feasible that T10-SP-AgNCs can serve as a facile and single-probe for dual-mode sensing of trace Hg2+.

The possible response mechanism of Hg2+ in T10-SP-AgNCs nanoprobe system were further investigated via XPS, circular dichroism (CD) spectra, fluorescence spectra, and HRTEM. As depicted in Fig. 3C, two new characteristic peaks of Hg0 arise at 99.5 eV and 102.5 eV (inset of Fig. 3C), which can be ascribed to the redox reaction between Hg2+ and Ag0 [39]. As shown in Fig. 3D, the deconvolution of XPS spectra for T10-SP-AgNCs gave two distinct components: the characteristic peaks of 368.88 eV (Ag 3d5/2) and 374.68 eV (Ag 3d3/2) were attributed to Ag+, and the typical peaks of 367.78 eV (Ag 3d5/2) and 373.78 eV (Ag3d3/2) were assigned to Ag0 [40,41]. The peak area ratio of Ag0/Ag+ in T10-SP-AgNCs is 8.08, indicating silver atom is the main component in the AgNCs (top of Fig. 3D). After adding Hg2+ into AgNCs, the peak area ratio of Ag0/Ag+ remarkably decreases from 8.08 to 2.48 (bottom of Fig. 3D). Furthermore, the CD spectra of T10-SP-AgNCs in the absence and presence of Hg2+ are shown in Fig. 3E, and the insignificant difference between two CD spectra reveals that Hg2+ cannot cause a remarkable conformation change of ssDNA-AgNCs. That is to say, T-Hg2+-T structure is not the main mode of Hg2+ in nanoprobe system. Moreover, the average particle size of T10-SP-AgNCs increases to 4.3 nm in the presence of trace Hg2+ (Fig. 3F) in comparison with that in the absence of Hg2+ (Fig. 2C). The Hg2+-triggered larger particle size of T10-SP-AgNCs may be attributed to the redox reaction between Hg2+ and Ag0 and subsequent formation of Ag0-amalgam nanoparticles (Ag/Hg NPs) [40].

Furthermore, EDTA was used to clarify the response mechanism of Hg2+ in T10-SP-AgNCs nanoprobe system (Fig. 3G and 3H), and the reaction process and possible fluorescence response mechanism of nanoprobe were deduced as Scheme 2 . The presence of Hg2+ intensively weakens the red fluorescence and simultaneously stimulates the green fluorescence of nanoprobe (green dashed curves of Fig. 3G). When EDTA was subsequently added after Hg2+ addition, the red fluorescence of nanoprobe was slightly weaken, and the green fluorescence was almost entirely quenched (blue dash-dot curves of Fig. 3G). It was inferred that the red emission of nanoprobe originated from the fluorophore containing high proportion of Ag0, and the green emission originated from the fluorophore with high percentage of Ag+. The prior addition of Hg2+ triggered redox reaction with Ag0 as well as amalgam, causing a decreased proportion of Ag0 and an increased percentage of Ag+ in nanoprobe system. Thus Hg2+ resulted in the decreased red fluorescence and the increased green fluorescence. Due to the strong coordination reaction between EDTA and Ag+, the subsequent addition of EDTA took away Ag+ from the surface of nanoprobe, and thus quenched both red and green fluorescence. The corresponding process and fluorescence evolution were illustrated in Scheme 2A. On the contrary, when EDTA was firstly added into nanoprobe system, both red and green fluorescence of nanoprobe rapidly decrease (red solid curves of Fig. 3H) because of the strong coordination reaction between EDTA and Ag+. Subsequent addition of Hg2+ just resulted in slight decrease of red-emission and a minor increase of green-emission (blue dash-dot curves of Fig. 3H). It was deduced that subsequent Hg2+ can be mostly chelated by EDTA, and a small amount of Hg2+ was captured by poly(T) tail and then further reacted with adjacent Ag0 on the surface of AgNCs. Thus subsequent addition of Hg2+ only caused a slight decrease of Ag0 and a minor increase of Ag+ on the surface of nanoprobe, coinciding with a decreased red fluorescence and an increased green fluorescence. The latter reaction process and fluorescence evolution were illustrated in Scheme 2B.

On the basis of above experimental results and the previous reports [38,39], the response process of Hg2+ in T10-SP-AgNCs nanoprobe system was plotted with black line in Scheme 2A, and the possible reaction equation is proposed in Scheme 2C. AgNCs nanoprobe contains abundant Ag0 on the surface of red-emission fluorophore and the abundant Ag+ on the surface of green-emission fluorophore. Flexible T10 tail in template ssDNA sequence closed to the red-emission fluorophore which possesses high proportion of Ag0. Hg2+ can easily be captured by T10 base due to the affinity interaction of T-Hg2+-T, and thus Hg2+ closes to red emitter. Then Ag0 on the surface of red emitter is rapidly oxidized by Hg2+ to generate Hg0 and Ag+, and the resultant Hg0 further integrates with Ag0 to form larger-size Ag/Hg NPs (Scheme 2A). Therefore, Hg2+ submits to the rapid redox reaction with Ag0 instead of coordination bonding with terminal T10 in ssDNA skeleton [42]. Therefore, trace Hg2+ can rapidly decrease the proportion of Ag0 and simultaneously increase the percentage of Ag+ on the surface of AgNCs nanoprobe. Consequently, trace Hg2+ rapidly quenches red fluorescence and synchronously increases green fluorescence of nanoprobe (Fig. 3A), thereby causing the fluorescence color change of nanoprobe from red to green under UV lamp (Inset of Fig. 3A). Moreover, Hg2+-induced larger-size Ag/Hg NPs on the surface of nanoprobe resulted in the color change from pink to pale yellow under visible light (Inset of Fig. 3B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimal conditions for the preparation of nanoprobe and the fluorescence sensing of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental condition generating maximum intensity of red-emission fluorescence was considered as optimal value for the preparation of T10-SP-AgNCs nanoprobe. Firstly, the effects of pH, temperature, buffer solution type, and the ratio of AgNO3 to NaBH4 on fluorescence intensity (FI) of nanoprobe were investigated. As shown in Fig. S2, the red fluorescence intensity is maximum when the buffer medium is Tris-HAc (Fig. S2A) and pH of Tris-HAc buffer solution is 6.6 (Fig. S2B). When the preparation temperature was 25 °C (Fig. S2C) and the mole ratio of AgNO3 to NaBH4 was 2:1 (Fig. S2D), the red fluorescence intensity reaches maximum. Furthermore, the reaction temperature and reaction time of Hg2+ in nanoprobe system were optimized. The condition causing maximum change of fluorescence intensity was considered as optimal value for sensing Hg2+. When reaction temperature was 25 °C (Fig. S2E), the decrement of red fluorescence (ΔFRed) and the increment of green fluorescence (ΔFGreen) reach maximum, respectively. When reaction time was 5 min (Fig. S2F), both the decreased red fluorescence (FIRed) and the increased green fluorescence (FIGreen) reached a plateau and remained stable at least 30 min. In brief, T10-SP-AgNCs was prepared in pH 6.6 Tris-HAc buffer solution at 25 °C and the molar ratio of AgNO3 to NaBH4 was 2:1. Then the optimal T10-SP-AgNCs was used as single probe for sensing Hg2+ after reaction at 25 °C for 5 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of the dual-mode ratiometric and visual sensor for Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: T10-SP-AgNCs as a single probe was used for the dual-mode ratiometric and visual sensing of Hg2+ under the optimal experimental conditions. Firstly, fluorescence mode of ratiometric and visual sensor was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the red fluorescence signal of nanoprobe at 626 nm sharply decreased, meanwhile the green fluorescence intensity at 535 nm gradually increases with increasing concentration of Hg2+ (C Hg 2+) (Fig. 4A). The fluorescence intensity ratio (F 626/F 535) is linear dependent on C Hg 2+ in the range of 0.5–175.0 nM with a detection limit of 0.2 nM, and the linear regression equation is Y = −0.1075C Hg 2+ + 19.46 (R2 = 0.9949) (Fig. 4B). The corresponding fluorescence photographs of nanoprobe in the presence of Hg2+ with different concentration were recorded under 365 nm UV lamp (Inset of Fig. 4A). Nanoprobe exhibits a wide fluorescence color change from red to yellow and green when increasing C Hg 2+, and Hg2+ as low as 10.0 nM can be discerned with the naked eye. Furthermore, colorimetric mode of ratiometric and visual sensor was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 4C, the absorbance of nanoprobe at 550 nm sharply decreases, meanwhile the absorbance at 430 nm gradually increases with increasing C Hg 2+. The relative peak height method has been used to measure A 430 and A 550. The detailed measure illustration and measure results have been presented in Fig. S3 and Table S1, respectively. The ratio (A 550/A 430) is linear dependent on C Hg 2+ in the range of 10.0–175.0 nM with a detection limit of 3.0 nM, and the linear regression equation is Y = −0.014C Hg 2+ + 3.194 (R2 = 0.991) (Fig. 4D). The corresponding colorimetric photographs of nanoprobe in the presence of Hg2+ with different concentration were recorded under visible light (Inset of Fig. 4C). The nanoprobe shows obvious color change from red to pale yellow with increasing C Hg 2+, and 75.0 nM Hg2+ can be identified with the naked eye, which is suitable for monitoring Hg2+ in waste water. Consequently, the resultant ratiometric sensor exhibits low detection limit of both fluorimetry and colorimetry, and the fluorescence sensor enables the rapid visual monitoring Hg2+ in drinkable water according to the limit of Hg2+ from United States Environmental Protection Agency (<10.0 nM) [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, repeatability and stability of dual-mode ratiometric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the selectivity of the proposed ratiometric sensor, the fluorescence and colorimetric response of nanoprobe solutions containing Hg2+ and possible interfering substances were investigated under optimal experimental conditions. The possible interfering substances with certain concentration causing a ±5 % relative error was considered as the tolerance limit. As shown in Fig. 5 A, only Hg2+ causes a remarkable change of fluorescence intensity and fluorescence color change under UV lamp (Inset of Fig. 5A), while the other metal ions with high concentration show no any interference. Similarly, the colormetric response of nanoprobe was tested, and only Hg2+ resulted in the obvious change of absorbance (Fig. 5B) and color change under visible light (Inset of Fig. 5B). Thus the proposed dual-mode ratiometric sensor exhibits excellent selectivity and naked-eye discrimination ability for Hg2+. The excellent selectivity AgNCs probe for Hg2+ can be ascribed to the difference in the standard redox potential between the Hg2+/Hg (0.85 V) and Ag+/Ag (0.8 V) [43].

Furthermore, the repeatability of the proposed ratiometric sensor was evaluated based on dual-mode sensing of 125.0 nM Hg2+. As exhibited in Fig. S4A and B, the relative standard deviation (RSD) of three replicated fluorimetric and colorimetric experiments ranges from 1.93 % and 3.20 %, indicating the good repeatability of sensor. Moreover, the AgNCs nanoprobe showed stable fluorescence and colorometric response at least 3 days (Fig. S4C and D). By comparison with the previous methods for Hg2+ assay (Table S2), the proposed dual-mode ratiometric and visual sensor showed a superior analytical performance including facile sensor construction, simple operation, rapid ratiometric response, visual sensing, high sensitivity, good selectivity, and wide linear range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practicability of the proposed sensor for Hg2+ in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Several environmental water samples were collected and used to further assess the practicability of the resultant dual-mode sensor for Hg2+ assay. As shown in Table S3, Hg2+ cannot be detected in tap water and river water samples. Recovery studies were conducted by spiking Hg2+ standard solution into water samples. The detection results of both fluorimetry and colorimetry were no significant difference. The average recoveries of Hg2+ were in the range of 100.1–108.3 %, and the RSDs were below 5 %. ICP-MS, a reliable method for accurate analysis of Hg2+ [44], was also used to detect the Hg2+ in these real water samples, and detection results were listed in Table S3. These results based on resultant dual-mode ratiometric senor were consistent with those using ICP-MS. These results demonstrated the developed dual-mode ratiometric optical sensor is practical and reliable for detection of Hg2+ in drinking water and environmental samples.

In conclusion, this work develops a novel ssDNA-templated AgNCs with dual-emission fluorescence by regulating terminal base sequence and type in ssDNA template. AgNCs was employed as a single probe to construct label-free and dual-mode ratiometric sensor for rapid visual sensing of trace Hg2+. We also reasonably deduce the response mechanism of Hg2+ in nanoprobe system. Due to the specific redox reaction and physical interaction between Hg2+ and Ag0 on the surface of AgNCs, the resultant sensor exhibited convenient operation, rapid response, superior selectivity, high sensitivity and accuracy for Hg2+. The study not only inspires the design of multi-emission ssDNA-templated AgNCs by adjustable terminal ssDNA sequence, but also offers a new single-probe and dual-mode ratiometric sensing platform for Hg2+. The proposed design strategy can be easily extended to ratiometric sensing of other analytes.

Yu Zou: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Ying Zhang: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition. Hui Zhu Wang: Investigation. Meng Wei Jiang: Investigation. Guo Feng Gui: Data curation. Dong Fu: Data curation. Wang Ren: Methodology, Supervision, Validation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work received support from the Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province (No. 2022NSFSC0103), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22064005), the Key Laboratory of Low-cost Rural Environmental Treatment Technology at Sichuan University of Arts and Science Education Department of Sichuan Province (XCH2022ZB-04), the Undergraduate Innovation Foundation of Sichuan Province (S20221062209), and the Innovation Fund of Postgraduate, Sichuan University of Science & Engineering (Y2023058, Y2024080).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.125751.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Mao, C. Wei, Cytosine-rich ssDNA-templated fluorescent silver and copper/silver nanoclusters: optical properties and sensitive detection for mercury(II), Microchim. Acta 186 (2019) 541.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dot synthesis of a Ag/PCQD composite for the visual detection of Hg2+ in dH2O solution and seawater samples
New J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.L. Wu, X.H. Li, Y. Liu, Y.L. Leng, Y. Li, X.H. Cai, Carbon quantum dot synthesis of a Ag/PCQD composite for the visual detection of Hg2+ in dH2O solution and seawater samples, New J. Chem. 46 (46) (2022) 22213-22221.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive and selective ratiometric fluorescent and visual detection of Cu2+ based on the hydroxytyrosol-naphthoresorcin-quantum dots sensing platform
Microchem. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.Q. Cheng, Z.H. Dai, H.X. Gao, Q.S. Pan, X.J. Kong, F.F. Shen, S. Wu, Highly sensitive and selective ratiometric fluorescent and visual detection of Cu2+ based on the hydroxytyrosol-naphthoresorcin-quantum dots sensing platform, Microchem. J. 195 (2023) 109450.
